text: The thoracoscopic view reveals the vertebra, esophagus, arch of the azygos vein, trachea beneath the peritoneal layer, subclavian vessel at the superior aspect, intercostal spaces, rib indentations, and diaphragm.

The surgeon makes a small incision on the dorsal aspect of the peritoneum using a monopolar hook, allowing carbon dioxide to enter and expand the surgical plane. A fatty structure is visible superior to the tip of the hook, indicating the location of the thoracic duct.

Carbon dioxide insufflation opens up avascular spaces for dissection. Small vessels, direct branches from the aorta, are visible on the posterior aspect of the dissection field. A small vessel near the tip of the hook instrument is dissected, while larger vessels may require a bipolar or vessel sealer.

The surgeon continues the dissection by incising the peritoneum on the dorsal aspect, using traction to allow carbon dioxide to enter and open the surgical plane. A larger vessel, identified as a direct branch from the aorta, is visible. The surgeon uses a hook to dissect around the vessel and employs a Ligasure vessel sealer to manage the larger vessel.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to effectively seal a vessel, preventing retractile bleeding. The carbon dioxide insufflation continues to dissect the space, guiding the direction for further dissection. Energy is applied to ensure no larger vessels remain, and attention is turned to mobilizing and releasing the peritoneum towards the hiatus.

The surgeon halts dissection just before completely separating the hiatus from the thoracic region to prevent carbon dioxide from escaping into the abdominal cavity. The patient is positioned prone, with working and camera ports established in the fifth, sixth, and seventh intercostal spaces.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to dissect and take down the inferior pulmonary ligament through three 5mm ports, with a 5mm telescope providing the view. As the dissection progresses, the lung parenchyma becomes visible, prompting careful reorientation to avoid injury to the lung tissue.

The surgeon adjusts traction and counter traction near the lung parenchyma to clearly identify the dissection area. The small peritoneum between structures is taken down to release the inferior pulmonary ligament, facilitating esophagus mobilization. Traction by the left hand opens avascular planes, minimizing bleeding. The dissection approaches the pulmonary vein, visible posterior to the current area.

The surgeon identifies the pulmonary vein, visible with a bluish hue and transmitted pulsations from cardiac activity. An incision is made on the ventral side of the peritoneum, revealing the thin white vagus nerve below the hook. Dissection continues above the vagus nerve to mobilize the esophagus, with the trachea visible posterior to the dissection area.

The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum using cautery, avoiding deeper tissue to prevent injury to the trachea, which appears white and collapsed in the surgical field. The dissection is performed in avascular planes, ensuring minimal bleeding while the trachea is visible inferior to the working area.

The surgeon approaches the arch of the azygos vein during dissection, dividing the peritoneum and surrounding tissues up to the arch. The trachea is visible, expanding as the dissection progresses. The surgeon holds the arch of the azygos and performs dissection behind it to release peri-esophageal tissues, aiming for en bloc mobilization of the esophagus below the azygos vein.

The surgeon uses carbon dioxide insufflation to guide the dissection beyond the esophagus, revealing the transmitted pulsations of the heart and the pericardium. The loose areolar tissues are sharply dissected with a hook, opening up the planes and allowing visualization of the pericardium from the opposite side, indicating that the esophagus has been encircled.

The surgeon performs ventral dissection near the pericardium, visible with transmitted pulsations from cardiac activity causing movement of the esophagus. Traction is applied by the assisting hand, while short bursts of cautery are used to meticulously open tissue planes. The left lung is visible through a thin, translucent pleura, showing a blackish discoloration.

The surgeon provides counter traction on the esophagus using an atraumatic instrument, ensuring the pleura remains intact to avoid opening the opposite side. The dissection continues with a monopolar hook, targeting small branches without encountering major blood vessels.

The surgeon uses a low-profile hook cautery instrument to dissect and mobilize the esophagus, carefully releasing peri-esophageal tissues while maintaining the integrity of the pleura. The edge of the pleura is clearly visible, and dissection proceeds towards the cranial end, ensuring the pleura remains intact.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to open avascular planes near the left lung, revealing the thin pleura membrane. The peri-esophageal tissues are identified, and the vagus nerve is visible on the left side, with the hook pointing towards the pleura.

The surgeon carefully avoids the clearly defined edge of the pleura while performing dissection towards the cranial aspect. The peritoneum is incised and released to mobilize the esophagus in a circular manner from the hiatus to the thoracic inlet. The vagus nerve is visible, and traction with counter traction is applied to guide further dissection. Carbon dioxide aids in opening the planes, revealing the edge of the esophagus being retracted with an atraumatic instrument for continued dissection.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection, revealing fatty tissue and small branches from the aorta. Using short, secure strokes, the dissection is carried out meticulously, avoiding thick tissue coagulation. Blunt traction helps push tissues aside, and a small branch is dissected using cautery. The left bronchus becomes visible beyond the dissection area, with thin avascular tissues being carefully separated. The aorta is clearly seen above the surgical instrument.
The surgeon navigates a narrow space with careful control of the dissection instrument to avoid injury to vascular structures. The left bronchus is visible just beyond the current area of dissection.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the cranial portion, using short bursts of cautery to release fibrous attachments around the esophagus. Traction is applied to open up the planes, revealing the pericardium and allowing removal of lymphovascular tissues surrounding the esophagus.

The surgeon applies traction to pull the esophagus towards the working port, revealing the pericardium. The edge of the pleura is clearly visible, and care is taken to avoid injuring it. The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to dissect the tissues around the pleura.

The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to meticulously dissect the fatty tissue between the left pleura and the esophagus, clearly visualizing the edge of the pleura and the bar pericardium. The lymphovascular tissues are held and dissected, progressing towards removal with the specimen.

The surgeon meticulously dissects fatty tissue surrounding the pericardium, using traction and counter traction to expose the area. The pericardium appears bare, with no tissues or structures over it. A blackish structure, identified as the infracranial group of lymph nodes, becomes visible, typically black in patients with a smoking history.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the esophagus, avoiding direct manipulation of the reactive lymph nodes to prevent bleeding. The nodes appear soft and tend to ooze under traction. Dissection progresses on the ventral aspect of the esophagus, revealing the pulmonary vein on the opposite side.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook in short bursts to release fibrous attachments around the esophagus, making gradual progress in the dissection. The surgical field is focused on the pulmonary vein, with small branches visible in the area, requiring careful and meticulous dissection to avoid bleeding and maintain clear visibility of the anatomical planes.

The surgeon removes the last of the fatty tissue, revealing the pericardium on the right side. The esophagus is being mobilized, with small adhesions on the ventral aspect being released. Lifting the esophagus exposes the translucent dissected area on the opposite side, while small fibrous attachments are sharply dissected using a monopolar hook.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the caudal end, removing fatty tissue over the pericardium along with the specimen. The limits of the dissection are clearly identified, and the esophagus is retracted dorsally to facilitate further dissection on the ventral side. Flimsy tissues are present, which are easily dissected using a combination of blunt and sharp techniques with a monopolar hook.

The surgeon shifts focus to the cranial end of the esophagus, dissecting the area between the bronchus and the pericardium on the right side. The intracranial lymph node group is visible and dissected for sampling. The vagus nerve is clearly visible, and fibrous attachments on the fatty tissue are being taken down along with the specimen.

The surgeon identifies small vessels surrounding the esophagus, with no major structures in the immediate area. The membranous portion of the trachea becomes visible, and the surgeon carefully releases fibrous attachments between the lymph node and the trachea on the left side using a monopolar hook. The white structure visible is the trachea, indicating proximity to critical anatomy.

The laparoscopic view shows the membranous trachea and left bronchus as the surgeon carefully dissects lymph nodes adjacent to these structures. The surgeon makes progress in removing lymphovascular structures, gradually incorporating the entire lymph nodal block into the specimen.

The surgeon provides traction and counter-traction near the bronchus to facilitate dissection of nodal and fatty tissues. The bronchus is clearly visible, with the bifurcation into left and right branches prominently displayed. The trachea is seen superiorly as a single structure.

The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to dissect the final portion of tissue close to the left bronchus. The fleshy tissues are visible, with small vessels running through the area. The surgeon carefully dissects the remaining fibrotic attachments holding the lymph node in place near the left bronchus.

The surgeon carefully dissects fibers near the membranous portion of the trachea using monopolar energy, ensuring a safe distance of at least five millimeters from the bronchus and trachea to prevent thermal spread. Traction is applied to separate the area from the bronchus, and small vessels are meticulously coagulated for hemostasis. The subcarinal group of lymph nodes is mostly dissected, with only a small portion remaining at the bifurcation level.

The surgeon applies traction to the esophagus, using the fixed membranous portion of the trachea for counter traction, facilitating safe dissection. The esophagus is mobilized, with remaining dissection near the right bronchus. Periesophageal tissues are manipulated to avoid direct handling of the esophagus. Lymphovascular tissues on the anterior aspect between the esophagus and pericardium are dissected and removed, revealing the pericardium clearly. The vagus nerve becomes visible during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects below the bifurcation level to avoid disturbing the vagus nerve, ensuring the recurrent laryngeal nerve is not injured. Black-colored lymph nodes are visible, and the surgeon avoids directly holding them due to their friable nature and tendency to ooze. Instead, traction is applied to surrounding tissues like the lung and esophagus to expose clear dissection planes. Lymphovascular structures are identified, and surrounding tissues are held to facilitate excision of the entire tissue along with the specimen.

The surgeon divides fibrotic strands and small vessels, enhancing the mobility of lymph nodes and surrounding structures for specimen removal. Using a hook, tissues are lifted bit by bit, while the lung and bronchus are pushed aside to increase the safety margin for lymph node clearance near the right bronchus. The pulmonary vein is visible, showing pulsations, with the pericardium lateral to it. Small vessel branches are meticulously coagulated and divided to ensure a secure procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects small lymph nodes near the bifurcation, ensuring they are included with the specimen. The membranous portion of the bronchus is visible as it joins the trachea. Short bursts of cautery are used intermittently, minimizing fumes and reducing the risk of lateral thermal spread.

The surgeon identifies the dissection plane between the vagus nerve and peri-esophageal tissue to mobilize the esophagus up to the thoracic inlet. The bronchus bifurcation from the trachea is visible, and the surgeon carefully works near the membranous portion of the trachea, ensuring traction does not inadvertently lift or injure these structures during cautery use.

The surgeon uses carbon dioxide insufflation to reveal the avascular planes for dissection, applying traction and counter-traction to expose the bifurcation area. Lymph nodes near the bifurcation are carefully dissected using a vessel sealing device, ensuring minimal thermal spread to the membranous portions of the bronchus and trachea. Suction is used to clear the area and precisely identify the bronchus limits, avoiding injury.

The surgeon leaves a small cup of normal tissue near the bronchus to prevent thermal injury during dissection. Suction is used to clear oozing, ensuring the bronchus's extent is well identified. Lymphovascular structures are excised with the specimen, followed by coagulation and division of small branches and fibrotic tissues. Dissection occurs on the left bronchus side, below the bifurcation, to safely detach lymphovascular tissue.

The surgeon carefully suctions blood and mobilizes the esophagus laterally, avoiding injury to the left bronchus. The bifurcation level is cleared of lymphovascular structures, with remaining tissue tethered to the left bronchus being dissected using a ligature. The final attachments to the pericardium are released, completing the en bloc dissection of the infracarinal portion. The pericardium and left bronchus are clearly visible.

The surgeon suctions the area to check for bleeding, confirming no major bleeding or oozing is present. The lymphovascular tissues have been completely cleared. The dissection proceeds to the supra-azygus portion, where the peritoneum is incised to mobilize the esophagus towards the thoracic inlet. The arch of the azygus is clearly visible, providing space for ligation.

The surgeon carefully dissects the esophagus, creating a clear plane while avoiding injury to the azygos vein. The right bronchial artery is noted to be in close proximity. A window is created above the trachea, and the azygos vein is ligated from the dorsal to the ventral side using a square knot, ensuring a secure ligation with a small stump left on both the ascending and descending portions. The vein visibly collapses after ligation.

The surgeon uses a suture passer to manipulate the vein, stretching the stump for ligation. Scissors are used to divide the vein, leaving a small cuff on either side to ensure the knot remains secure. The superior portion opens up, revealing the dissection planes aided by carbon dioxide insufflation. The surgeon holds the edge of the peritoneum, applying traction and counter traction to visualize the surgical field in three dimensions.

The surgeon observes the bulging and pulsation of the subclavian vessels, with the superior vena cava visible laterally. Lymphovascular tissues and peritoneum are held and incised to complete dissection up to the thoracic inlet. The lung is collapsed due to pneumothorax, and the peritoneum is dissected over the trachea at the superior aspect of the azygos arch.

The surgeon lifts the esophagus, carefully dissecting and removing small vessels and fibrotic strands between the trachea and esophagus. The right bronchial artery is identified and preserved, visible as it is pushed by the tip of the hook. The telescope orientation is adjusted to provide a clear view of the bronchial artery throughout the dissection.

The surgeon identifies and dissects fibrotic strands near the pulsating right bronchial artery, exercising caution to avoid inadvertent cautery injury to nearby structures such as the aorta and trachea. The dissection continues on top of the trachea to mobilize the esophagus in the supra-azygus portion, ensuring the esophagus is free for subsequent manipulation. The vagus nerve is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon cauterizes small branches at the OG junction level, ensuring meticulous dissection to enhance tissue mobility. The bronchiol artery is visible with clear pulsation, indicating careful preservation during the procedure.

The surgeon holds tissue around the bronchial artery, revealing its entire course. Minimal fibrous tissue is dissected to ensure the bronchial artery is clearly visible. The membranous portion of the trachea appears collapsed and begins to inflate with positive pressure from the ventilator.

The surgeon dissects small fibrotic strands between the esophagus and trachea, completing the mobilization of the esophagus from the thoracic portion, ensuring no further dissection is needed in this area when proceeding to the neck.

The surgeon dissects lymphovascular tissues located on top of the trachea, which are to be taken along with the esophagus. The subclavian artery is clearly visible, with the left recurrent laryngeal nerve hooking over it. Below the subclavian, the Superior vena cava is identified by its bluish hue, with the nerve running clearly on top. This marks the completion of the thoracoscopic dissection phase.
An ICD tube is placed to reduce pneumothorax, visible in the post-operative setting.
